topic: A person learns their favorite restaurant is closing
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sign, faded now to a ghostly white, mocked me from the corner of the street. I stood there, my hand gripping the cold metal railing of the awning, the familiar scent of caramelized onions and aged wine suddenly feeling alien, sharp in the humid evening air. My mind felt like a skipping record, the melody of my routine fractured, leaving only static. I kept blinking, trying to pull the reality of the closing announcement back into focus, but the edges kept blurring. "They're finally shutting down," I mumbled, the sound thin and reedy. I turned away, the pavement suddenly too rough beneath my worn shoes, and walked aimlessly toward the subway, every step a conscious effort to anchor myself to something solid.

[story 2]

I stared at the empty maître d' station, the polished wood gleaming under the harsh sodium lights. Every familiar detail—the chipped blue tile on the floor, the way the bartender wiped down the bar—seemed to vibrate with a wrongness. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I reread the final notice tacked onto the door. It was a dizzying sensation, like being underwater, the pressure building behind my eyes. I tried to call a friend, but the words caught in my throat, lodging there like grit. I took a step back, bumping against a nearby potted plant, and let out a small, involuntary sound—a soft, wounded sigh—before forcing my feet to move again, dragging myself toward the exit.

[story 3]

The menu, usually a comforting map of culinary delights, seemed to swim before my eyes. I traced the outline of the signature dish with my finger, the ink blurring slightly. A strange lightness settled in my chest, a feeling of unmooring, as if gravity had temporarily forgotten its job. I kept looking toward the entrance, expecting the door to swing open, expecting the familiar clatter of silverware, but there was only silence. My jaw felt heavy, slack. "No," I whispered, shaking my head slowly, as if trying to clear water from my ears. I looked around the dining room, at the vacant tables, and the world felt tilted, spinning just slightly off its axis. I needed to move, needed a direction, but my legs felt weighted with lead.
